title: Ash serving tray with cut-out handles
subtitle: A flat tray cut from a single ash board, with oval cut-out handles at each end
excerpt: A flat ash serving tray, 450 mm by 320 mm, with 100 mm oval cut-out handles at each short end. Cut from a single 18 mm ash board, with the handles shaped using a coping saw, chisel, and spokeshave. Oiled to a smooth, food-safe finish. About half a day's work for a confident woodworker.
sourceNotes: Victorian kitchen and domestic craft manuals describe wooden trays and salvers of this kind, including Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics, volume III (1885).

content.text: A flat ash serving tray, 450 mm by 320 mm, with oval cut-out handles at each short end. No joinery, just careful shaping: the trick is a top face flat enough to hold a glass without rocking, and handle openings shaped clean and comfortable to grip. About half a day's work with the kit you already have if you do wood work, plus drying time for the finish.
The
spokeshave
is the tool that does the work inside the handle opening that a plane cannot reach. Keep both hands behind the cutting edge and clamp the tray blank to the bench before coping-saw or chisel work.
Materials
One seasoned ash board, 450 mm by 320 mm by 18 mm. Sanding sealer and food-grade oil for finishing.
Method
Cut the tray
Saw the tray blank to size, 450 mm by 320 mm, and square all four edges.
Mark the handle opening at each short end: an oval 100 mm by 45 mm, centred, 30 mm from the end edge and 30 mm from each long side.
Drill a 12 mm starter hole inside each marked oval, then cut around the outline with a coping saw.
Shape the handles
Pare the long sides of each oval with a bevel-edge chisel.
Work the curved ends with a flat-bottomed spokeshave, taking light cuts with the grain. The inside surface should be smooth and free of saw marks before finishing.
Ease all edges with a light chamfer using a chisel or a few passes of 120-grit sandpaper.
Finish the surface
Plane the top face flat with a smoothing plane, working diagonally and then with the grain. Finish with a card scraper.
Ash is open-grained. Apply one coat of sanding sealer and rub back with 320-grit paper before oiling, to get the
grain-filling
that makes the surface food-safe and easy to wipe clean.
Brush on two coats of food-grade oil, letting the first soak in and dry before applying the second.

items.fix: Reverse direction at the midpoint of each curved end: approach the tight curve from one side, then the other. The spokeshave cuts cleanly only when moving with the grain.
items.cause: Spokeshave taken against the grain on the curved end of the handle, fibres torn rather than cut
items.symptom: Handle edge feels rough under the hand even after spokeshave work
